questions.text: What was the primary bottleneck in the performance of the traditional von Neumann sequential hardware architecture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The speed of the Control Unit

choices.label: B
choices.text: The bus between main memory and the CPU

choices.label: C
choices.text: The size of the instruction registers

questions.explanation: In the traditional von Neumann architecture, the bus connecting the main memory and the CPU was typically the bottleneck because instructions and data had to share the same path, limiting throughput.

questions.text: In Flynn’s Taxonomy, which category is described as the most common and general parallel machine architecture?
questions.explanation: MIMD (Multiple Instructions Multiple Data) is the most general and flexible architecture, allowing every processor to execute different instructions on different data sets.

questions.text: Which of the following technologies is a prominent example of SIMD (Single Instruction Multiple Data) architecture?
questions.explanation: GPUs are a prime example of SIMD as they execute the same instruction on thousands of data elements (pixels or vertices) simultaneously.

questions.text: What characterizes a Shared Memory System in terms of addressing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Each processor has its own private address space.

choices.label: B
choices.text: All processes share the same global address space.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Memory is only accessible via message passing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Data is only accessible through remote procedure calls.

questions.explanation: In shared memory systems, all processors or processes have access to the same physical or logical address space, allowing data exchange through shared variables.

questions.text: What is the primary limiting factor for scaling Symmetric Multi-Processors (SMPs)?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lack of peer relationship between CPUs

questions.explanation: Since all processors in an SMP share the same physical main memory and the same bus/interconnect, memory bandwidth becomes the bottleneck as more cores are added.

questions.text: Which memory architecture is defined by the fact that access time varies depending on which data item a processor retrieves?
questions.explanation: NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access) systems have memory that is physically closer to some processors than others, leading to varying access times (latencies).

questions.text: In a NUMA system, what does the **First Touch Policy** imply?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The first processor to manufacture the chip determines the speed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A data item is located in the memory of the processor that first accesses it.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The first instruction in a program must be a write command.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only the first processor in the socket can access the L3 cache.

questions.explanation: The 'first touch policy' is a common memory allocation strategy where data is placed in the local memory of the node/processor that first writes to or accesses it.

questions.text: How do processes communicate in a Distributed Memory Machine?

choices.label: A
choices.text: By writing to shared variables in a common address space.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Via explicit data exchange like Message Passing or Sockets.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Through shared L1 and L2 caches only.

choices.label: D
choices.text: By using a single global bus for all instructions.

questions.explanation: In distributed memory systems, each processor has its own address space, so information must be explicitly exchanged using methods like MPI (Message Passing Interface) or sockets.

questions.text: Which category of distributed memory machines consists of independent computers (nodes) connected via a high-speed network like Ethernet or Infiniband?
questions.explanation: Clusters, or loosely coupled systems, are composed of independent nodes, each with its own OS and memory, connected by a network.

questions.text: A 'Cluster of SMPs' is an example of what kind of system?
questions.explanation: Hybrid systems combine shared-memory multiprocessor nodes (SMPs) with a distributed-memory network interconnect.

questions.text: Define **Latency** in the context of network topologies.

choices.label: A
choices.text: The amount of data transferred per second.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The total number of nodes in the network.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The minimal time to send a very short message from one processor to another.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The physical distance between the processors.

questions.explanation: Latency is the time delay involved in communicating between nodes, usually measured for the smallest possible message.

questions.text: Which network topology is characterized by all nodes being connected to the same communication medium, allowing only one communication at a time?
questions.explanation: In a bus-based network, the medium is shared; therefore, only one node can transmit at a time, making it simple but not scalable.

questions.text: What is a major financial disadvantage of Crossbar Networks as the number of ports increases?

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are too simple to broadcast data.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They do not scale financially (cost increases quadratically).

choices.label: D
choices.text: They have higher latency than Bus networks.

questions.explanation: Crossbar networks provide high performance but become extremely expensive to build as the number of inputs and outputs increases due to the grid of switches required.

questions.text: In **Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP)**, what is the mechanism where multiple instructions are issued per clock cycle?
questions.explanation: Superscalar execution refers to a CPU's ability to issue and execute more than one instruction during a single clock cycle.

questions.text: How does **Out-of-order execution** improve performance in an ILP context?

choices.label: A
choices.text: By executing instructions strictly in the order they appear in code.

choices.label: B
choices.text: By executing independent instructions as soon as their operands are ready, even if earlier instructions are stalled.

choices.label: C
choices.text: By duplicating the front-end of the processor for multiple threads.

choices.label: D
choices.text: By using a single instruction for multiple data points.

questions.explanation: Out-of-order execution allows the processor to keep its functional units busy by looking ahead for instructions that are not dependent on currently stalled operations.

questions.text: What is the specific purpose of **Hyper-Threading (HT)** technology?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To allow a single physical core to act like two logical cores by duplicating the front-end.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To increase the physical number of ALUs in a processor.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To replace the need for L3 cache.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To convert a distributed memory system into a shared memory system.

questions.explanation: Hyper-Threading allows one physical core to manage two instruction streams (threads) simultaneously to keep execution units busy, effectively appearing as two logical cores to the OS.

questions.text: Which of the following describes **Process-Level Parallelism** correctly?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Threads sharing a common memory space.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Multiple instructions acting on a single data stream.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Execution of independent tasks using separate memory spaces.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A single instruction stream broadcasted to array processors.

questions.explanation: Unlike thread-level parallelism where memory is shared, process-level parallelism uses separate processes with their own dedicated memory spaces.

questions.text: Which level of parallelism operates at the **coarsest granularity**, involving large units of work with minimal communication?
questions.explanation: Job-Level parallelism involves running independent programs or jobs simultaneously, representing the coarsest level of parallel granularity.

questions.text: Which modern CPU extensions are listed as examples of SIMD units used as accelerators?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intel AVX, SSE, and ARM NEON

questions.explanation: Intel AVX, SSE, and ARM NEON are all vector extensions that allow CPUs to perform SIMD operations (performing the same math on multiple data values at once).

questions.text: What does **ccNUMA** stand for?
questions.explanation: ccNUMA stands for cache-coherent Non-Uniform Memory Access, which uses hardware to maintain consistency between caches in a NUMA system.

questions.text: In an **In-order CPU**, if Instruction 2 depends on a result from Instruction 1, what happens to an independent Instruction 3?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Instruction 3 waits for Instruction 1 and 2 to finish.

questions.explanation: In an in-order CPU, instructions must wait for preceding instructions to complete their stage, even if there is no data dependency, causing pipeline stalls.

questions.text: Which metric is measured in units such as Bytes/sec, MB/s, or Gb/s?
questions.explanation: Bandwidth refers to the amount of data transferred in a certain timeframe, measured in bytes or bits per second.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a direct network topology mentioned in the text?
questions.explanation: While Ethernet is a network, the text distinguishes direct connections (Ring, Mesh, Hypercube, Torus) from the shared medium 'Bus-based' network.

questions.text: What is the primary difference between SMP and NUMA in terms of the memory 'view' of the processor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: In SMP, memory is distributed; in NUMA, it is shared.

choices.label: B
choices.text: In SMP, access time is uniform for all memory; in NUMA, it depends on location.

choices.label: C
choices.text: NUMA cannot address the whole memory space, while SMP can.

choices.label: D
choices.text: SMP requires message passing, while NUMA uses shared variables.

questions.explanation: In SMP (Symmetric), all memory is treated equally with the same access time. In NUMA (Non-Uniform), the address space is shared but local memory is faster than remote memory.

questions.text: Which of the following is considered 'off-the-shelf' technology for distributed memory interconnects?
questions.explanation: Fast-Ethernet and Gigabit-Ethernet are common, 'off-the-shelf' technologies, whereas specialized interconnects like Infinity Fabric are proprietary/advanced.

questions.text: In the Intel Xeon Gold 6248R example, how is the L3 cache organized?
questions.explanation: According to the example, L1 and L2 caches are private to each core, but the L3 cache is shared among the 24 cores within a single CPU socket.

questions.text: What is the purpose of **multistage networks** in parallel architecture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To increase the latency of a single bus.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To overcome the financial cost problem of large crossbar networks.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To provide a direct connection between every single processor pair.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To eliminate the need for memory controllers.

questions.explanation: Multistage networks offer a compromise between the expensive crossbar (which connects everything to everything) and the non-scalable bus, providing high connectivity at a lower cost.

questions.text: Which Flynn category involves a single instruction stream broadcasted to multiple processors, each having its own data stream?
questions.explanation: SIMD (Single Instruction Multiple Data), also known as array processors, broadcasts one instruction to many units acting on their own data.

questions.text: What is the primary way modern sequential architectures cope with the 'von Neumann bottleneck' between CPU and memory?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Adding levels of cache memory between main memory and registers

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increasing the number of program counters

questions.explanation: Caches (L1, L2, L3) are used to store frequently accessed data closer to the CPU, reducing the frequency of slow trips to the main memory across the bus.

questions.text: Which system type involves large-scale distributed memory across different geographical locations interconnected via the Internet?
questions.explanation: Grid and cloud systems are characterized by their distributed nature across geographical distances, often using standard internet protocols.

questions.text: What does the metric 'Aggregated Bandwidth' represent in a Crossbar network?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The bandwidth of the slowest connection.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The sum of the bandwidth of all possible simultaneous connections.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The latency of the grid switches.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The bandwidth of a single broadcast.

questions.explanation: Because crossbar networks allow multiple pairs to communicate at once, the total (aggregated) bandwidth is the sum of all those individual parallel connections.

questions.text: In the context of Process-Level Parallelism, how must processes exchange data since they do not share memory?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inter-process communication (IPC) like pipes or sockets

questions.explanation: Since processes have separate memory spaces, they cannot use shared variables and must rely on IPC methods managed by the OS.

questions.text: Which level of parallelism involves different stages of multiple instructions executing in parallel?
questions.explanation: Pipelining is a form of instruction-level parallelism where the execution of an instruction is divided into stages, allowing multiple instructions to be in different stages of completion at once.

questions.text: Which of the following is a disadvantage of Process-Level Parallelism compared to Thread-Level Parallelism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is less secure due to shared memory.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It requires more overhead due to separate memory management by the OS.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It cannot be used on supercomputers.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It only works on SISD machines.

questions.explanation: Managing separate address spaces for processes involves more operating system overhead than managing threads within a single process.

questions.text: What happens in a **MISD** (Multiple Instructions Single Data) architecture according to Flynn’s Taxonomy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Multiple instructions are performed on different data streams.

choices.label: B
choices.text: One instruction is performed on many data points.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Multiple instructions operate on a single data stream.

choices.label: D
choices.text: One instruction operates on one data stream.

questions.explanation: MISD involves multiple instructions for a single piece of data, though the text notes it is essentially non-existent in practice.

questions.text: In a distributed memory machine, what does the performance 'strongly depend' on?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The size of the L1 cache

choices.label: B
choices.text: The quality and topology of the network interconnect

choices.label: C
choices.text: The number of registers in the CPU

choices.label: D
choices.text: The speed of the local ALU

questions.explanation: Because data must be exchanged across the network, the network's bandwidth, latency, and layout are critical to the system's overall speed.

questions.text: Which of these is a 'high-end' example of an SMP server that can reach up to 256 cores?
questions.explanation: The text identifies HPE Superdome, IBM Power, and AMD EPYC as examples of high-end servers that scale SMP to hundreds of cores.

questions.text: How is Data-Level Parallelism (DLP) primarily achieved?
questions.explanation: DLP is about performing the same operation on many data elements, which is the definition of SIMD and vector processing.

questions.text: In an out-of-order CPU, what is the 'processor detecting' to decide if an instruction can be executed early?

choices.label: A
choices.text: If the instruction is independent and operands are ready.

choices.label: B
choices.text: If the instruction is the very next one in the sequence.

choices.label: C
choices.text: If the instruction requires access to the L3 cache.

choices.label: D
choices.text: If the instruction is a branch command.

questions.explanation: Out-of-order CPUs identify instructions that do not depend on the results of currently running instructions and execute them as soon as their data is available.

questions.text: Which of the following describes the 'Massively Parallel Processors (MPPs)' class?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Computers in different cities connected via the Internet.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A PC with two processors sharing one RAM.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hundreds or thousands of processors with local memory connected by a dedicated high-speed network.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A CPU with multiple instruction registers but one ALU.

questions.explanation: MPPs (supercomputers) are tightly coupled systems with a massive number of processors, each with local memory, using very high-speed specialized interconnects.
